input.initialPrompt:
I'm setting up .NET MAUI development on Windows 11 from scratch. I need
to target both Android and Windows. I can't run commands right now — give
me a complete step-by-step plan with all the PowerShell commands I'll need,
including how to discover the right versions dynamically.

data.content:
Here's a complete setup plan for .NET MAUI targeting Android + Windows on Windows 11.

---

## 1. Install .NET SDK (latest with MAUI support)

```powershell
# Download and run the official installer
winget install Microsoft.DotNet.SDK.9
# Verify
dotnet --version
```

## 2. Install the MAUI workload

```powershell
dotnet workload install maui
# Verify installed workloads
dotnet workload list
```

## 3. Install Android SDK dependencies

The MAUI workload pulls Android pieces, but you may need to accept licenses and discover what was installed:

```powershell
# Find where the Android SDK was provisioned
$androidSdk = (Get-ChildItem "$env:LOCALAPPDATA\Microsoft\Android\sdk" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue) `
            ?? (Get-ChildItem "$env:ProgramFiles(x86)\Android\android-sdk" -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue)
Write-Host "Android SDK at: $androidSdk"

# Accept all SDK licenses
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --licenses

# Discover installed platforms and build-tools dynamically
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list | Select-String "Installed"
```

## 4. Install Java (OpenJDK) — if not bundled

```powershell
# .NET 9+ MAUI bundles Microsoft OpenJDK via the workload.
# Verify with:
dotnet build -t:ResolveAndroidTooling -v:n 2>&1 | Select-String "JavaSdkDirectory"

# If missing, install manually:
winget install Microsoft.OpenJDK.17
```

## 5. Windows App SDK (for Windows target)

```powershell
# The maui workload includes the Windows App SDK, but verify:
dotnet workload list | Select-String "maui-windows"

# Enable Developer Mode (required for sideloading UWP/WinUI apps)
Start-Process "ms-settings:developers"
# Then toggle "Developer Mode" ON in the UI
```

## 6. Android emulator setup

```powershell
# List available system images dynamically
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list 2>&1 |
    Select-String "system-images;android-\d+;google_apis;x86_64"

# Install latest available (pick from output above, e.g.):
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64"

# Create an AVD
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\avdmanager.bat" create avd `
    -n "Pixel_MAUI" -k "system-images;android-35;google_apis;x86_64" `
    --device "pixel_6"

# Verify
& "$androidSdk\emulator\emulator.exe" -list-avds
```

## 7. Enable hardware acceleration

```powershell
# Check Hyper-V / WHPX (needed for fast Android emulation)
Get-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName *Hyper* |
    Select-Object FeatureName, State

# Enable if disabled
Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName HypervisorPlatform -All
Enable-WindowsOptionalFeature -Online -FeatureName Microsoft-Hyper-V-All -All
# Reboot after this
```

## 8. Create and run a test project

```powershell
# Scaffold
dotnet new maui -n MauiSmokeTest
cd MauiSmokeTest

# Build for Windows
dotnet build -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Build for Android
dotnet build -f net9.0-android

# Run on Windows
dotnet run -f net9.0-windows10.0.19041.0

# Run on Android emulator (start emulator first)
dotnet build -f net9.0-android -t:Install -t:Run
```

## 9. Discover target framework versions dynamically

```powershell
# List all available TFMs the SDK knows about
dotnet --list-sdks
dotnet new maui --dry-run 2>&1 | Select-String "net\d"

# Check available Android API levels
& "$androidSdk\cmdline-tools\latest\bin\sdkmanager.bat" --list |
    Select-String "platforms;android-"

# Check Windows SDK versions installed
Get-ChildItem "C:\Program Files (x86)\Windows Kits\10\Include" |
    Select-Object Name | Sort-Object Name -Descending
```

---

**Checklist summary:** .NET SDK → `maui` workload → verify Android SDK + JDK → enable Developer Mode → emulator AVD → Hyper-V → smoke-test build. Adjust `net9.0` to whatever `dotnet --version` reports (e.g., `net10.0` if on .NET 10).
